P. 12(b)(1), Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6), Am. Int’l Underwriters (Philippines), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253 (9th Cir. 1988); and Fed. R. Civ. P. 41(b), move the Court for an order dismissing Plaintiff’s First Amended Complaint (“FAC”) and Second Amended Complaint (“SAC”)
cited: Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(1), Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6), Am. Int’l Underwriters (Philippines), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253 (9th Cir. 1988), Fed. R. Civ. P. 41(b)

quoted_point: Even if this Court decides Plaintiff may have a second opportunity to litigate his claims in this Court, the SAC violates the statute of limitations, was filed without authority, and fails to state a claim upon which relief can be granted.

on the following grounds: (1) the amended complaints are repetitive to Plaintiff’s Complaint previously filed in Oregon State Court and constitutes an improper attempt to remove his state court action; (2) the amended complaints violate the statute of limitations; (3) Plaintiff lacked authority to file his amended complaints; (4) Section 1983 claims against the Clackamas County Sheriff’s Office are not raised against a proper entity; (5) Plaintiff failed to properly state a claim upon which relief can be granted under the Due Process Clause of the 14th Amendment; (6) Plaintiff failed to properly state a claim upon which relief can be granted for his alleged Section 1983 Claims directed at County Defendants; and (7) Plaintiff failed to plead compliance with the Oregon Tort Claims Act.

Plaintiff filed amended complaints.

The following defendants were dismissed for want of prosecution: Clackamas County Police Department, Clackamas County John Doe Police Officer No. 1, Clackamas County John Doe Police Officer No. 2, County of Clackamas, Clackamas County Jail, and Rebecca Portlock.
The only defendants to appear were West Linn Police Department and West Linn Police Department Officers Dana Gunnerson and Caitlin Blyth.
West Linn Defendants filed a motion to dismiss and motion for summary judgment.

Eight days later, Plaintiff filed a motion to dismiss his case without prejudice because he wanted to pursue his claims in federal court.

On May 23, 2024, Plaintiff filed a Notice of Removal in this Court to remove his case described above, Clackamas County Circuit Court Case No. 22CV39627.
cited: U.S. District Court of Oregon, Case No. 3:24-cv-00838-SB, ECF No. 2-1

quoted_point: On May 23, 2024, the same day Plaintiff filed his Notice of Removal, Plaintiff commenced this action by filing his original complaint.

Plaintiff’s claims for relief included a claim for negligence, deprivation of property with our Due Process, “Civil Theft or Conversion,” “Violation of Civil Rights,” and Intentional Infliction of Emotional Distress.
Plaintiff’s complaint identified the following defendants: State of Oregon, County of Clackamas, Clackamas County Police Department, John Doe Officer 1, and John Doe Officer 2.

According to the FAC, Plaintiff’s claims relate to alleged “property destruction” by a person named “Macy” between March 4 and 6, 2022, his arrest, his conditions of confinement and medical care while detained on related criminal charges, his criminal prosecution, and the timing of his release from jail on July 8, 2022.

On August 7, 2024, the Court adopted Magistrate Judge Beckerman’s Findings and Recommendations (ECF No. 9) and dismissed the complaint sua sponte; dismissing claims against the State of Oregon with prejudice due to Eleventh Amendment immunity, and dismissing claims against the remaining Defendants without prejudice for failing to state a claim for relief.
Plaintiff then filed a FAC on September 9, 2024.
Regarding his claim surrounding medical treatment received at Clackamas County Jail, Plaintiff alleges that his requests for contact lenses and proper eyewear for his “legal blindness” were denied.

The Court’s Order authorized Plaintiff to amend his original Complaint concerning claims against Clackamas County, the Clackamas County Police Department, and John Doe Officers 1 and 2 within 30 days.

The FAC identified the following defendants: Clackamas County, West Linn Police Department, West Linn Police Department Officer Dana Gunnarson, West Linn Police Department Officer Catlin Blyth, Clackamas County Sheriff’s Department, Clackamas County Jail, Deputy District Attorney Rebecca Portlock, John Doe 1, and John Doe 2.

The FAC purports to state nine causes of action, including (1) unlawful detection, (2) malicious prosecution, (3) conspiracy to deprive civil rights, (4) failure to prevent harm, (5) cruel and unusual punishment, (6) false imprisonment, (7) deliberate indifference, (8) intentional interference with contractual relations, and (9) a Monell claim for failure to train and official custom of deliberate indifference.
Moreover, Plaintiff requested the consolidation of claims from his previously dismissed (unidentified) cases.
On October 9, 2024, Plaintiff filed a Motion for Leave to File Second Amended Compliant that included the SAC.

On September 12, 2024, Plaintiff filed a Notice to the Court, in pertinent part, requesting the tolling of the statute of limitations due to “significant delays caused by both state and federal (court) procedural barriers. As well as Plaintiff’s legal blindness and the lack of ADA accommodations.”
On September 24, 2024, the Court ordered Plaintiff not to further amend his complaint without leave of the Court.
Plaintiff also requested his SAC to relate back to the date of his original filing in May 2024, pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 15(c).
cited: Fed. R. Civ. P. 15(c); ECF No. 17

quoted_point: Plaintiff sought to include additional evidence, “inadvertently omitted from the First Complaint,” regarding his legal blindness that impacts his ability to represent himself and review documents, and to correct factual claims and constitutional provisions.

The SAC generally concerns alleged incidents between March 4 and 6, 2022, Plaintiff’s arrest on March 6, 2022, his incarceration at the Clackamas County Jail between March 6 and July 8, 2022, and his criminal prosecution that resulted in a dismissal of all criminal charges.
A motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(1) is appropriate when a complaint lacks subject matter jurisdiction.
cited: Chandler v. State Farm Mut. Auto. Ins. Co., 598 F.3d 1115, 1122 (9th Cir. 2010)

quoted_point: On February 3, 2025, the Court issued a Notice of Lawsuit and Request for Waiver of Service to Clackamas County Sheriff’s Office, Clackamas County, Deputy District Attorney Rebecca Portlock, Clackamas County Jail, Unknown Persons 1 and 2, West Linn Police Department, Officer Dana Gunnarson, and Officer Catlin Blyth.
Rule 12(b)(1) motions to dismiss concerning subject matter jurisdiction may be challenged either facially or factually.
cited: Safe Air for Everyone v. Meyer, 373 F.3d 1035, 1039 (9th Cir. 2004)

quoted_point: The County Defendants waived service for Clackamas County, the Clackamas County Sheriff’s Office, and the Clackamas County Jail.

The SAC purports to bring eleven claims pursuant to 42 U.S.C. § 1983 (Fourth Amendment – Unlawful Arrest, Fourth Amendment – Malicious Prosecution, Fourth Amendment – Conspiracy, Fourteenth Amendment – Failure to Protect, Fourteenth Amendment – Cruel and Unusual Punishment, Fourteenth Amendment – False Imprisonment, Fourteenth Amendment – Deliberate Indifference to Medical Needs, Monell Claim for Failure to Train and Supervise, Seventh Amendment – Denial of Right to Civil Jury Trial, Fifth Amendment – Violation of Due Process, Sixth Amendment – Right to Counsel and Fair Trial) and a single tort claim for intentional interference with contractual relations.
A facial challenge is limited to a review of the allegations in a complaint, while a factual challenge is supported with extrinsic evidence.
cited: Safe Air for Everyone v. Meyer, 373 F.3d 1035, 1039 (9th Cir. 2004)

quoted_point: A motion to dismiss under Rule 12(b)(6) is appropriate when a plaintiff has “failed to state a claim upon which relief can be granted.”
Generally, complaints must be pled with specificity.
cited: Bell Atlantic v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 555 (2007); Iqbal v. Ashcroft, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009)

quoted_point: If the facts alleged in a complaint show that a claim is barred by the statute of limitations, a party may bring a Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim.
cited: Jablon v. Dean Witter & Co., 614 F.2d 677, 682 (9th Cir. 1980)

quoted_point: Jurisdiction “must be rejected if there is any doubt as to the right of removal in the first instance.”
the Ninth Circuit continues to apply the “benefit of liberal construction” to pleadings filed pro se.
cited: Rishor v. Ferguson, 822 F.3d 482, 495 (9th Cir. 2016); Butler v. Long, 752 F.3d 1177, 1180 (9th Cir. 2014)

quoted_point: To dismiss the complaint as untimely, it must appear beyond doubt that the plaintiff cannot establish the timeliness of a claim.
cited: Supermail Cargo, Inc. v. United States, 68 F.3d 1204, 1207 (9th Cir. 1995)

quoted_point: when the plaintiff is pro se, the documents filed must be liberally construed.
cited: Erickson v. Pardus, 551 U.S. 89, 94 (2007); Roy v. Lampert, 465 F.3d 964, 970 (9th Cir. 2006)

quoted_point: “A plaintiff who commences his action in a state court cannot effectuate removal to a federal court even if he could have originated the action in a federal court and even if a counterclaim is thereafter filed that states a claim cognizable in a federal court.”
cited: Oregon Egg Producers v. Andrew, 458 F.2d 382, 383 (9th Cir. 1972)

quoted_point: “The right to remove a state court case to federal court is clearly limited to defendants.”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Philippines), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1260 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: 28 U.S.C. § 1441(a) limits the authority to remove a case from state to federal court to a defendant or defendants: “Except as otherwise expressly provided by Act of Congress, any civil action brought in a State court of which the district courts of the United States have original jurisdiction, may be removed by the defendant or the defendants, to the district court of the United States....”
A plaintiff cannot achieve the same result of removal by dismissing its state court case without prejudice and then refiling the case in federal court.
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Philippines), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1260 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: A plaintiff has no right to bring multiple cases based on the same facts.
cited: Reyes v. Bolles, No. 2:19-cv-02108-AC, 2020 WL 129090, at *2 (D. Or. Jan. 10, 2020)

quoted_point: A plaintiff is not permitted to remove its case to federal court.
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Philippines), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1260 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: In AIU, the plaintiff first filed an action in state court, then, after two-and-a-half years of litigation, the plaintiff filed suit in federal district court “alleging the same facts and claims.”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Philippines), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1255 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: The district court granted and the Ninth Circuit affirmed the decision to grant the defendant’s motion to dismiss for “the avoidance of duplicative litigation.”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Philippines), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1256 (9th Cir. 1988)

Courts analyze separately standing for declaratory and injunctive relief claims because a plaintiff must demonstrate standing separately for each form of relief sought.
cited: Ctr. for Biological Diversity v. Mattis, 868 F.3d 803, 815 (9th Cir. 2017)

quoted_point: In addition to seeking monetary and punitive damages, Plaintiff seeks declaratory and injunctive relief.

Plaintiff has no current or future involvement with City Defendants. Therefore, Plaintiff lacks standing to seek any injunctive relief as there is no plausible allegation of a real or immediate threat that Plaintiff will suffer any further harm by City Defendants.
cited: City of Los Angeles v. Lyons, 461 U.S. 95 (1982)

quoted_point: They are used to “afford relief from the uncertainty, insecurity, and controversy giving rise to the proceeding,” or for parties to obtain an adjudication of their rights before actual injuries or losses have occurred.
cited: Bilbrey by Bilbrey v. Brown, 738 F.2d 1462, 1470 (9th Cir. 1984); J.M. v. Major, 2018 WL 7104882, at *2

quoted_point: To have standing to seek injunctive relief, a plaintiff must demonstrate a likelihood of repeated injury or future harm to the plaintiff in the absence of the injunction.
cited: City of Los Angeles v. Lyons, 461 U.S. 95, 103 (1982); Imagineering v. Kiewit Pac. Co., 976 F.2d 1303, 1308-09 (9th Cir. 1992)

quoted_point: Typically, requests for declaratory relief target prospective conduct.
cited: J.M. v. Major, No. 6:18-CV-00739-YY, 2018 WL 7104882, at *2 (D. Or. Oct. 2, 2018); Green v. Mansour, 474 U.S. 64, 73 (1985); Bilbrey by Bilbrey v. Brown, 738 F.2d 1462, 1470 (9th Cir. 1984)

quoted_point: “malicious, wanton, or oppressive acts or omissions are within the boundaries of traditional tort standards for assessing punitive damages and foster ‘deterrence and punishment over and above that provided by compensatory awards.’ . . . Such acts are therefore all proper predicates for punitive damages under § 1983."
cited: Dang v. Cross, 422 F.3d 800, 807 (9th Cir. 2005); Smith v. Wade, 416 U.S. 30, 49 (1983)

quoted_point: Claims seeking retrospective declaratory relief are superfluous when they are intertwined with a claim for money damages.
cited: J.M. v. Major, 2018 WL 7104882, at *3; McQuillion v. Schwarzenegger, 369 F.3d 1091, 1095 (9th Cir. 2004)

quoted_point: In order to state a claim for punitive damages, plaintiff must allege sufficient facts that either “defendant’s conduct was driven by evil motive or intent, or when it involved a reckless or callous indifference to the constitutional rights of others.’”
Plaintiff must be seeking more than a retrospective opinion that he was wrongly harmed by City Defendants.
cited: J.M. v. Major, 2018 WL 7104882, at *2-*3; Green v. Mansour, 474 U.S. 64 (1985)

quoted_point: Plaintiff only offers conclusory opinions that City Defendants’ actions were “willful and reckless”. (ECF No. 17, p. 89.)

Plaintiff’s Second Amended Complaint fails to allege any facts that City Defendants acted with “evil motive or intent,” acted with a “reckless or callous indifference,” or were “malicious, wanton, or oppressive” in conduct.
cited: Morgan v. Woessner, 997 F.2d 1244 (9th Cir. 1993); Dang v. Cross, 422 F.3d 800 (9th Cir. 2005)

quoted_point: the issuance of a declaratory judgment would have much the same effect as a full-fledged award of damages or restitution by the federal court.

The same is true here with regard to Plaintiff’s request for retrospective relief. (ECF No. 17, p. 90.) Like the case in J.M., these requests cover only past conduct. Plaintiff also seeks compensatory and punitive damages for this same conduct. Therefore, Plaintiff’s claims for declaratory relief should be dismissed.
cited: ECF No. 17, p. 90; J.M. v. Major, 2018 WL 7104882

quoted_point: These baseless statements fall far short of the pleading requirements under Iqbal and Twombly in order to state a claim for punitive damages, and therefore the claim should be dismissed.
cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662 (2009); Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544 (2007)

cited: Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6); Court’s Order dated Aug. 7, 2024 (ECF No. 12)

quoted_point: This Complaint was dismissed by the Court on August 7, 2024, in its entirety, however the Court granted Plaintiff leave to “amend the claims against Clackamas County, the Clackamas County Police Department, and John Doe Officers 1 and 2 within 30 days.” (ECF No. 12.)

“Factual allegations [in the complaint] must be enough to raise a right to relief above the speculative level, . . . on the assumption that all the allegations in the complaint are true (even if doubtful in fact).”
cited: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 555

quoted_point: A complaint must contain factual allegations that state a claim “that is plausible on its face.”
In assessing “plausibility,” the court must draw on its “judicial experience and common sense” and consider “obvious alternative explanation[s].”
cited: Cafasso, U.S. ex rel. v. Gen. Dynamics C4 Sys., Inc., 637 F.3d 1047, 1056 (9th Cir. 2011)

quoted_point: It is not sufficient that allegations “conceivably” could state a claim; the theory must, in fact, be “plausible.”

A “pleading that offers ‘labels and conclusions’ or a ‘formulaic recitation of the elements of a cause of action will not do’… Nor does a complaint suffice if it tenders ‘naked assertion[s] devoid of further factual enhancement.’”
A complaint must give the defendant fair notice of what the claim is and the grounds upon which it rests.
cited: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 555 (2007)

quoted_point: All claims now brought by Plaintiff based upon these alleged facts are barred by the applicable statute of limitations.

All of Plaintiff’s claims against City Defendants are made against a public body or its employee while acting within the scope of his duties and are therefore subject to the two (2) year statute of limitations.
Any allegations made against City Defendants for tortious acts that occurred prior to October 9, 2022, are plainly outside of the prescribed two-year statute of limitations.
As such, all claims against City Defendants are barred by the two-year statute of limitations and City Defendants are entitled to summary judgment as a matter of law.
attempts to amend a complaint to assert an untimely claim will not be permitted to be “distorted by the parties to circumvent the limitations period.”
cited: DW Aina Le'a Dev., LLC v. Haw. Land Use Comm'n, 2025 U.S. App. LEXIS 3762, *4 (9th Cir. 2025); United States ex rel. Wulff v. CMA, Inc., 890 F.2d 1070, 1075 (9th Cir. 1989)

quoted_point: causes of action arising under 42 U.S.C. § 1985 are subject to the limitations period of analogous state personal injury claims
cited: Taylor v. Regents of Univ. of Cal., 993 F.2d 710, 711-12 (9th Cir. 1993)

quoted_point: district courts do not abuse their discretion when they find the amended complaint does not relate back to the filing of the original complaint and therefore committed no error in dismissing the amended complaint.
cited: Kilkenny v. Arco Marine, Inc., 800 F.2d 853, 858 (9th Cir. 1986)

All actions complained of were during the life of the criminal case, post-arrest criminal charging, and prior to the dismissal of the criminal case and therefore she is entitled to absolute immunity.
At times plaintiff complains about ex parte communication between DDA Portlock and the judge, and at other times he includes his attorney as a “conspirator”.
Despite plaintiff’s attempts, statements made in court by the prosecutor acting as an advocate in a judicial proceeding qualify for absolute immunity.

Further, as mentioned above, DDA Portlock is entitled to absolute immunity for statements made in court during a criminal proceeding and the facts supporting plaintiff’s allegation of collusion or conspiracy are conflicting and confusing.
In the complaint plaintiff alleges that DDA Portlock “lied to the judge”.
It appears the “conspiracy” was the prosecutor and plaintiff’s attorney advising the court in a hearing that plaintiff tested positive for COVID-19, resulting in a continuance of plaintiff’s trial.
It is unclear how that constitutes conspiracy if the court is not also colluding.

Plaintiff alleges that DDA Portlock fabricated evidence, however in support of those allegations, he cites statements made in court by DDA Portlock resulting in the continuance of plaintiff’s criminal trial. (Pl. Resp., ECF No. 38 at 56-57.)
Plaintiff further attempts to use the court hearing referenced above as evidence of collusion or conspiracy between DDA Portlock, plaintiff’s attorney advisor, and the court.
Statements made by a prosecutor in court are immune from liability regardless of whether plaintiff believes the statements to be accurate.

“A prosecutor gathering evidence is more likely to be performing a quasi-judicial advocacy function when the prosecutor is ‘organiz[ing], evaluati[ng], and marshaling [that] evidence” in preparation for a pending trial, in contrast to the police-like activity of “acquiring evidence which might be used in a prosecution.’”
According to plaintiff, the witness made exculpatory statements at a hearing then the witness left the area to be unavailable to testify further.
Even if the witness did feel pressured in some way, plaintiff fails to allege how he was harmed.
Finally, even if there was some harm, DDA Portlock’s actions are fully immune from liability.
However, the courts have explained that witness interviews can be investigatory or advocacy-related.

It should be noted that plaintiff cites to Rose v. Bartle, 871 F.2d 331 (3d Cir. 1989) to support his position that a conspiracy might overcome prosecutorial immunity. (ECF No. 38 at 60.) Like many of plaintiff’s other cites, that is an inaccurate representation of what the case stands for. Rose supports DDA Portlock’s position. Even if she were to try to use false testimony to convict plaintiff, it would be covered by absolute immunity.
cited: Rose v. Bartle, 871 F.2d 331, 344-45 (3d Cir. 1989); ECF No. 38 at 60

quoted_point: Plaintiff argues that DDA Portlock failed to disclose exculpatory evidence, but again, even if that did occur, she has absolute immunity.

Plaintiff claims there was “tampering” with a witness, however the nature of those allegations are not supported by facts and it further appears that no harm came to plaintiff even if those events did occur.
Plaintiff claims this witness contact by DDA Portlock falls outside her absolute immunity.
Plaintiff’s allegations involving an alleged witness for the criminal case are also nonspecific and unclear.
The fact that DDA Portlock or her office may have contacted a witness during the pendency of a criminal case is not improper, much less a liable offense.
There was no trial and the criminal case was dismissed.

TYLER ALLEN LOFALL, Plaintiff,

Additionally, an alleged disability alone does not per se entitle Plaintiff to equitable tolling; rather, he must show that his "impairment was so severe that . . . [he] was unable to rationally or factually understand the need to timely file, or . . . [his] mental state rendered him unable to personally prepare a habeas petition."
As the forum state, in Oregon, equitable tolling is “only available when circumstances outside the control of the affected party make it impossible to comply with a statute of limitations."
cited: Allen v. Nw. Permanente, P.C., 2013 WL 865967, at *6 (D. Or. Jan. 2, 2013)

quoted_point: Equitable tolling is not available to plaintiffs in order to avoid the consequence of their own negligence and does not apply when a late filing is due to claimant's failure to exercise due diligence.
cited: Giustina Land & Timber Co., LLC v. Eugene Water & Electric Bd., 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 82009, *17 (D. Or. Apr. 30, 2025)

quoted_point: However, this doctrine is used “sparingly” in Oregon.
cited: Kobold v. Good Samaritan Reg'l Med. Ctr., 832 F.3d 1024 (9th Cir. 2016)

quoted_point: In order to establish equitable tolling applies, Plaintiff must demonstrate that that he has been pursuing his rights diligently; and that, despite this, some extraordinary circumstance stood in his way.
cited: Conn. v. Multnomah Co. Sheriff Dep't, 2025 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 51439, *9 (D. Or. Mar. 20, 2025)

quoted_point: Plaintiff appears to concede his claims are subject to a two-year statute of limitations. (ECF No. 38, p. 34.)

Plaintiff argues that this statute was tolled for various reasons, however, for the following reasons, there is no tolling provision applicable to Plaintiff’s claims and Plaintiff’s claims are therefore barred by the statute of limitations.

The diligence element requires proof of “effort that a reasonable person might be expected to deliver under his or her particular circumstances and asks whether the plaintiff was without any fault in pursuing his claim."
cited: Booth v. United States, 914 F.3d 1199, 1202 (9th Cir. 2019)

quoted_point: The second requires proof that the “extraordinary circumstances were the cause of his untimeliness and made it impossible to file the document on time."
cited: Booth v. United States, 914 F.3d 1199, 1202 (9th Cir. 2019)

quoted_point: In this case, there is no articulated basis which would warrant application of equitable tolling to the statute of limitations.
Plaintiff admits that he was able to file a complaint (with the same factual basis) in state court in November of 2022.

Similarly, issues related to incarceration will only constitute an extraordinary circumstance where there is demonstrable evidence that said incarcerable made it impossible to access the information or materials necessary to pursue a claim.
cited: Smith v. Cain, 2024 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 153136, *4-5 (D. Or. July 1, 2024)

quoted_point: This admission also demonstrates that his arguments related to disability and incarceration are without merit as well.

This admission demonstrates that there was no barrier to Plaintiff’s timely filing of the action and his tactical choices in terms of venue and filing numerous complaints in different venues does not vitiate that simple fact.

Those factors were equally present in November of 2022, and ostensibly did not prevent him from being able to pursue his claims, as such, there is nothing in the record which would permit an inference that these purported barriers justify the untimely (and redundant) filing in Oregon District Court two years later.
Accordingly, there is no merit to these arguments, and they should be disregarded.
Allegations that these alleged violations were undertaken pursuant to a policy or practice does not render them any less discrete and does not toll their accrual or extend the limitations period.
cited: Douglas v. Noelle, 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 134817; Lyons v. England, 307 F.3d 1092, 1108 (9th Cir. 2002)

quoted_point: For the same reasons discussed above, Plaintiff has utterly failed to demonstrate he was prevented from comprehending his rights such that tolling would apply.
However, based upon Plaintiff’s admission, he was successful in timely filing a lawsuit raising similar/same claims in state court over two years ago.
If this alleged conduct were to have delayed or prevented him from pursuing his claims in Oregon District Court, necessarily it would have presented an identical barrier in state court.
Plaintiff argues ORS 12.160 should toll his claims, however, this statute only applies when a Plaintiff’s mental condition barred him from comprehending the rights he is otherwise bound to know.
cited: Ogan v. Oregon Health & Sci. Univ., No. 3:17-CV-1849-SI, 2018 WL 2105378, at *2 (D. Or. May 7, 2018)

quoted_point: Plaintiff further argues that his claims constitute an ongoing pattern of violations which persisted, apparently, through the present.
Plaintiff makes various arguments that, in essence, City Defendants bad conduct tolled the statute of limitations.
Setting aside the spurious and conclusory nature of the allegations, Plaintiff’s arguments are again defeated by the procedural history of this case.

the Ninth Circuit has concluded that district courts do not abuse their discretion when they find the amended complaint does not relate back to the filing of the original complaint and therefore committed no error in dismissing the amended complaint.
cited: Kilkenny v. Arco Marine, Inc., 800 F.2d 853, 858 (9th Cir. 1986)

quoted_point: the procedural rules which permit amendments and adding parties were never “intended to assist a plaintiff who ignores or fails to respond in a reasonable fashion to notice of a potential party, nor was it intended to permit a plaintiff to engage in piecemeal litigation.”
cited: Kilkenny v. Arco Marine, Inc., 800 F.2d 853, 858 (9th Cir. 1986); Unicure, Inc. v. Thurman, 97 F.R.D. 1, 6 (W.D.N.Y. 1982); Gutierrez v. Raymond International, Inc., 86 F.R.D. 684, 685 (S.D. Tex. 1980)

quoted_point: these type of arguments are routinely dismissed, as the critical question is when the discrete acts, which are allegedly made pursuant to some kind of municipal policy or practice, occur.
cited: Douglas v. Noelle, 2010 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 134817, *7 (D. Or. Dec. 17, 2010)

quoted_point: “Discovery” for purposes of when the action accrues happens when a plaintiff is, or should be, aware of 1) the injury; 2) the cause of the injury; and 3) the identity of the tortfeasor.
cited: Raethke v. Oregon Health Scis. Univ., 115 Or. App. 195, 198 (1992)

quoted_point: a “cause of action for personal injury accrues from the date the injury is, or should have been discovered, not from the time the full extent of damages is ascertained.”
cited: Guiley v. Hammaker, 55 Or. App. 921, rev. den. 292 Or. 863 (1982)

quoted_point: even had Plaintiff sought amendment pursuant to FRCP 15(a)(2) as required, Plaintiff would not meet the standard required as Plaintiff has utterly failed to explain why he is only now, nearly four years after filing a nearly identical lawsuit in state court and having exchanged discovery in the state matter, seeking amendments to allegations against defendants in this matter.
FRCP 15(a)(2) does address the standard for making amendments to the complaint not as a matter of course – this does not somehow override the Court’s Order issued on August 7, 2024.

FRCP 15(a)(2) requires either the opposing party’s consent or the Court’s leave prior to any amended complaint being filed – Plaintiff had neither, accordingly, FRCP 15(a)(2) does not excuse Plaintiff’s violation of the Court’s prior order.

it is clear that Plaintiff had knowledge of "facts [that] would make a reasonable person aware of a substantial possibility that each of the three elements (harm, causation, and tortious conduct) exists."
It is irrelevant that the full scope of his claims and damages were purportedly unknown until more recently, as the critical elements were known to Plaintiff at the time they occurred.
Plaintiff concedes he violated this Court’s Order (see ECF No. 38, p. 63), but argues that he should nevertheless be permitted to amend his complaint pursuant to FRCP 15(a)(2).
cited: ECF No. 38, p. 63; Fed. R. Civ. P. 15(a)(2)

quoted_point: Plaintiff need not know each element with certainty, rather, Plaintiff must be aware of a "substantial possibility" of the existence of the relevant elements.
cited: Keller v. Armstrong World Indus., 197 Or. App. 450, 460-461 (2005)

quoted_point: Additionally, Plaintiff failed to respond to City Defendants arguments regarding his claims for declaratory and injunctive relief and punitive damages – as such, Plaintiff has conceded those claims, and they must be dismissed.
As such, Plaintiff has failed to state claims upon which relief can be granted.
Despite Plaintiff’s arguments to the contrary, his Complaint is rife with unsubstantiated allegations and conclusory statements without any further factual enhancement.
Given such circumstances, justice does not require Plaintiff be permitted to amend his pleadings and denying Plaintiff’s efforts would not constitute an abuse of discretion.
His allegations against City Defendants are pure conjecture on the part of Plaintiff and is utterly devoid of any factual support.

Now before the Court are Defendants’ motions to dismiss Lofall’s Second Amended Complaint.

Plaintiff Tyler Allen Lofall (“Lofall”), a self-represented litigant, filed this action against Defendants Clackamas County, Clackamas County Sheriff’s Office, Clackamas County Jail, unnamed Clackamas County Sheriff’s deputies (together, “County Defendants”), West Linn Police Department (“WLPD”), West Linn Police Officer Catlin Blyth (“Blyth”), West Linn Police Officer Dana Gunnarson (“Gunnarson”), and Deputy District Attorney Rebecca Portlock (“Portlock”) (all together, “Defendants”).
TYLER ALLEN LOFALL, Plaintiff,

[D]espite serving Clackamas County Sheriffs Dept, and the Jail multiple times prior to the 180 day deadline, the court refused to enforce the non-compliance. Moreover, . . . a DA in Washington County, defending the West Linn Defendants, received favoritism with untrue legal arguments at every pass. Consequentially, Lofall had all of his state claims dismissed for what appears to be Judicial Corruption from Clackamas County Judges again.
“[A]dverse rulings do not prove bias or conspiracy.”
cited: In re Complaint of Jud. Misconduct, 650 F.3d 1370, 1371 (9th Cir. 2011)

quoted_point: Aside from adverse rulings, Lofall fails to describe any grounds to support a finding that the Clackamas County Circuit Court would be an unfair venue to litigate his civil claims.

In his motion for guidance, Lofall suggests that the alleged misconduct during his criminal case and adverse rulings in his civil case “demonstrate[] that any trial conducted in Clackamas County would be fundamentally unfair.”
Lofall has demonstrated his ability to overcome any limitations that his self-represented status and alleged disability might pose.

Specifically, in November 2022, Lofall filed a 130-page complaint in state court in which he asserted constitutional claims against the same defendants. (See Compl., State Case (Or. Cir. Ct. filed Nov. 22, 2022).)
the Court’s website provides publicly available information that makes clear that plaintiffs “may file a complaint in person or by mail.”
cited: U.S. Dist. Ct. for the Dist. of Or., Handbook for Self‑Represented Parties, at 10 (2025), https://perma.cc/374N-CPH7

quoted_point: To the extent Lofall argues that his status as a self-represented plaintiff or his alleged disability constitute sufficiently “compelling reasons” to justify allowing him to circumvent the removal statute, the Court disagrees.

With respect to Lofall’s argument that “judicial corruption” in state court is a compelling reason to allow him to proceed in federal court, Lofall’s allegations relate to conduct that primarily occurred during Lofall’s criminal case, not his recently-dismissed civil case against Defendants. (See SAC at 33-34, alleging “judicial corruption” and “systemic judicial and state misconduct[.]”)

“In this case, substantive progress has been made in the two-and-a-half years of litigation in state court. The state court judge has decided seven motions and the parties have engaged in substantial discovery.”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1258 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: “On February 5, 2025, the Clackamas County Circuit Court scheduled a jury trial for August 26, 2025. Eight days later, Plaintiff filed a motion to dismiss his case without prejudice because he wanted to pursue his claims in federal court.”

“On November 18, 2022, Plaintiff initiated a civil action in Clackamas County Circuit Court. . . . [A] jury trial was scheduled for May 21, 2024. On May 20, 2024, Plaintiff filed a Notice of Removal to this Court, causing the trial to be canceled.”
“the policy considerations barring repetitive lawsuits have particular application when the state court litigation is at an advanced stage.”
cited: Protective Ins. Co. v. Iriana Ins. Servs., Inc., 2021 WL 8651845, at *6; Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1261 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: a plaintiff “should not be permitted to accomplish, by the refiling [in federal court] of its state court complaint, what would clearly be prohibited if [the plaintiff] tried to remove[.]”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1261 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: After Lofall’s attempted removal to federal court resulted in the cancelation of his state court trial, both his state case and new federal case were pending for several months until Lofall moved to dismiss the state case in February 2025 upon the state court’s next trial setting.
cited: Mot. Guidance at 7-8, ECF No. 21; City Mot. at 3, ECF No. 36

quoted_point: allowing Lofall to circumvent the removal statute to avoid a trial in state court after that court presided over his case from 2022 until 2025 is precisely the scenario the Ninth Circuit sought to avoid with its guidance in AIU.
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: Allowing Lofall to litigate his claims in state court until the eve of trial and then start over and litigate the same claims against the same defendants in federal court would waste judicial resources and result in prejudice to Defendants by duplicating the costs of defense.
Lofall acknowledges he “filed well over a hundred [] motions and engaged in extensive discovery efforts” in the state case prior to dismissal
(see Compl., relating only to Lofall’s alleged loss of property following a May 2022 arrest)

“[A] plaintiff may not file a lawsuit in state court and then file the same lawsuit in federal court. Filing the ‘repetitive lawsuit’ was an attempt to circumvent the federal removal statute, [Section] 1441, which does not grant a right of removal to plaintiffs.”
cited: In re Pac. Enters. Sec. Litig., 47 F.3d 373, 376 (9th Cir. 1995); 28 U.S.C. § 1441; Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1260-61 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: “[M]inor distinctions do not preclude a finding that the instant action and the Kansas state court action are parallel proceedings. . . . Therefore, the Court finds that this is a ‘repetitive lawsuit’—a parallel action brought by the same plaintiff in a second forum.’ As such, Plaintiffs ‘should not be permitted to accomplish, by the refiling of its state court complaint, what would clearly be prohibited if [Plaintiffs] tried to remove.’”
cited: Puckett v. New Day Broadband, LLC, No. 2:12-cv-01958, 2013 WL 4786380, at *3 (D. Nev. Sept. 5, 2013)

quoted_point: Accordingly, the Court dismisses this case without prejudice for lack of federal subject matter jurisdiction.
and the various equitable doctrines on which Lofall relies do not apply.
another reason not to allow this case to proceed in federal court is that all of the claims in Lofall’s SAC are time-barred
because more than two years elapsed before he pleaded those claims in this case,
dismissal is warranted under the Ninth Circuit’s decision in AIU, as Plaintiff’s commencement of the federal action represents an improper attempt to circumvent [Section] 1441
cited: Jackson Preyor Grp. v. Lima, No. 5:22-cv-00111, 2022 WL 17730382, at *7 (N.D. Cal. Nov. 10, 2022), aff’d, 2023 WL 8014351 (9th Cir. Nov. 20, 2023); 28 U.S.C. § 1441

quoted_point: For all of these reasons and consistent with Ninth Circuit authority, the Court finds that this is a repetitive lawsuit subject to dismissal and Lofall has failed to demonstrate “compelling reasons” to allow him to circumvent the removal statute and relitigate his state court case in federal court.
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253 (9th Cir. 1988); In re Pac. Enters. Sec. Litig., 47 F.3d 373 (9th Cir. 1995); Protective Ins. Co. v. Iriana Ins. Servs., Inc., 2021 WL 8651845; Transplace Int’l, Inc. v. Motorcar Parts of Am., 2015 WL 13285088; Puckett v. New Day Broadband, LLC, 2013 WL 4786380

quoted_point: the claims do not relate back to his original complaint
the Court does not reach the Rule 12(b)(6) arguments in Defendants’ motions because it lacks subject matter jurisdiction,

The cross-complaint filed by [one plaintiff] [in the state case] advances the same causes of action that Plaintiffs advance in this proceeding. It is also based on the same set of facts. Furthermore, the Ninth Circuit has explained that the policy considerations barring repetitive lawsuits have particular application when the state court litigation is at an advanced stage.
cited: Protective Ins. Co. v. Iriana Ins. Servs., Inc., No. 2:20-cv-07216, 2021 WL 8651845, at *6 (C.D. Cal. May 19, 2021); Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1261 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: This lawsuit is repetitive of the one Plaintiffs filed in state court and stayed [in state court]. Having elected to litigate in state court, Plaintiffs should be ‘bound by [their] choice absent compelling reasons to seek relief in another forum.’
cited: Transplace Int’l, Inc. v. Motorcar Parts of Am., No. 2:14-cv-07928, 2015 WL 13285088, at *6 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 6, 2015)

quoted_point: DATED this 3rd day of September, 2025.

IT IS SO ORDERED.
The Court dismisses the case without prejudice but without further leave to amend.

“To contest a plaintiff’s showing of subject matter jurisdiction, a defendant may file two types of Rule 12(b)(1) motions[.]”
cited: Dalfio v. Orlansky-Wax, LLC, No. 21-56339, 2022 WL 3083323, at *1 (9th Cir. Aug. 3, 2022)

quoted_point: “Under Rule 12(b)(1), a defendant may challenge the plaintiff’s jurisdictional allegations in one of two ways.”
cited: Leite v. Crane Co., 749 F.3d 1117, 1121 (9th Cir. 2014)

quoted_point: In addition, the “City Defendants incorporate by reference and join in [the County Defendants’] motion to dismiss (ECF No. 34) to the extent it is applicable to claims against [the] City Defendants, specifically, but not limited to [the] County Defendants’ arguments that Plaintiff’s Second Amended Complaint must be dismissed on the grounds that it is repetitive and constitutes an impermissible attempt to remove his (now dismissed) state court case.” (City Mot. at 2.)
Specifically, a defendant’s “Rule 12(b)(1) jurisdictional [challenges] can be either facial or factual.”
cited: San Diego Cnty. Credit Union v. Citizens Equity First Credit Union, 65 F.4th 1012, 1028 (9th Cir. 2023); White v. Lee, 227 F.3d 1214, 1242 (9th Cir. 2000)

quoted_point: a facial challenge “accepts the truth of the plaintiff’s allegations but asserts that they are insufficient on their face to invoke federal jurisdiction.”
cited: Bowen v. Energizer Holdings, Inc., 118 F.4th 1134, 1142 n.6 (9th Cir. 2024); Leite v. Crane Co., 749 F.3d 1117, 1121 (9th Cir. 2014)

quoted_point: City Defendants incorporate by reference and join in [the County Defendants’] motion to dismiss to the extent it is applicable to claims against City Defendants, specifically, . . . that Plaintiff’s Second Amended Complaint must be dismissed on the grounds that it is repetitive and constitutes an impermissible attempt to remove his (now dismissed) state court case.

Defendants move to dismiss Lofall’s claims on the ground that the Court lacks subject matter jurisdiction because Lofall “elected to proceed against Defendants in Oregon state court, and . . . [he] cannot undermine the limited removal authority in [28 U.S.C. §] 1441 by now filing a ‘repetitive lawsuit.’”
cited: County Mot. at 10, ECF No. 34; 28 U.S.C. § 1441

quoted_point: In light of Defendants’ reliance on the Court’s docket and other judicially noticeable materials in support of the Rule 12(b)(1) motion, the Court interprets the jurisdictional challenge as a facial challenge.

Lofall responds that his “state case was systematically obstructed, never reaching adjudication on the merits, and the present federal action represents his constitutional right to seek vindication of federal rights in a federal forum.”
The Ninth Circuit has “distinguish[ed] between ‘facial’ and ‘factual’ challenges to jurisdictional allegations in a complaint[,]”
cited: Jones v. L.A. Cent. Plaza LLC, 74 F.4th 1053, 1056 n.1 (9th Cir. 2023); Leite v. Crane Co., 749 F.3d 1117, 1121 (9th Cir. 2014)

quoted_point: Where, as here, the defendant asserts a facial challenge to jurisdiction, a district court evaluates that challenge under the “familiar Rule 12(b)(6) standard.”
cited: Bowen v. Energizer Holdings, Inc., 118 F.4th 1134, 1143 n.7 (9th Cir. 2024); Leite v. Crane Co., 749 F.3d 1117, 1121 (9th Cir. 2014)

quoted_point: “[A] plaintiff may not file a lawsuit in state court and then file the same lawsuit in federal court” because “[f]iling the ‘repetitive lawsuit’ . . . [would] circumvent the federal removal statute, [Section] 1441, which does not grant a right of removal to plaintiffs.”
cited: In re Pac. Enters. Sec. Litig., 47 F.3d 373, 376 (9th Cir. 1995); 28 U.S.C. § 1441

quoted_point: “[A] plaintiff who commences his action in a state court cannot effectuate removal to a federal court even if he could have originated the action in a federal court[.]”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1261 (9th Cir. 1988); Or. Egg Producers v. Andrew, 458 F.2d 382, 383 (9th Cir. 1972)

quoted_point: “[H]aving elected state court, plaintiff should be bound by its choice absent compelling reasons to seek relief in another forum.”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1261 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: “The Ninth Circuit has also held that Section 1441(a) bars a plaintiff from commencing a new federal action ‘based on the same facts and claims as the first suit’” because “[s]uch a ‘repetitive lawsuit’ seeks an end-run around the removal statute and is [therefore] subject to dismissal.”
cited: Protective Ins. Co. v. Iriana Ins. Servs., Inc., No. 2:20-cv-07216, 2021 WL 8651845, at *6 (C.D. Cal. May 19, 2021); Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1260-61 (9th Cir. 1988); In re Pac. Enters. Sec. Litig., 47 F.3d 373, 376 (9th Cir. 1995); 28 U.S.C. § 1441(a)

quoted_point: “The right to remove a state court case to federal court is clearly limited to defendants.”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1260 (9th Cir. 1988); 28 U.S.C. § 1441

quoted_point: The “codification[] of the removal statute . . . reflect[s] a Congressional intent that a plaintiff should not be permitted to alter the forum that it selects to litigate its claim against a particular defendant.”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1260-61 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: The parties have not identified, nor has the Court found, any case defining or analyzing “compelling reasons” that would justify allowing a repetitive lawsuit to proceed in federal court in circumvention of the removal statute.

“[M]inor distinctions do not preclude a finding that the instant action and the . . . state court action are parallel proceedings. . . . Therefore, the Court finds that this is a ‘repetitive lawsuit’—a parallel action brought by the same plaintiff in a second forum.’ As such, Plaintiffs ‘should not be permitted to accomplish, by the refiling of its state court complaint, what would clearly be prohibited if [Plaintiffs] tried to remove.’”
cited: Puckett v. New Day Broadband, LLC, No. 2:12-cv-01958, 2013 WL 4786380, at *3 (D. Nev. Sept. 5, 2013); Nakash v. Marciano, 882 F.2d 1411, 1416 (9th Cir. 1989); Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1260-61 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: “Lofall v. West Linn, et al., which arises from substantially identical facts and legal issues concerning the same underlying events of false arrest and malicious prosecution by some of the same defendants named in the federal action, as well as the additional defendants that flat out refused to be summoned two years prior, however they absolutely were put on notice and served.”

Lofall argues that “extraordinary circumstances” surrounding this case, including his self‑represented status, “significant visual disability[,]” and “allegations implicating fundamental constitutional rights . . . warrant maximum procedural flexibility.”
Lofall claims that he “was unable to file his claim directly in Federal Court due to a total loss of property while falsely arrested, including his ID and consequent access to the Federal Court.”
Lofall does not dispute that this case is based on the same facts and claims as his recently-dismissed state case.

Lofall further argues that Defendants’ misconduct, including perceived “judicial corruption” in his state case, justifies applying “the well-established doctrines of equitable tolling, fraudulent concealment, continuing violations, and relation back[.]”
The Court finds that this federal action is a “‘repetitive lawsuit’ . . . subject to dismissal absent compelling reasons to seek relief in another forum.”
cited: Protective Ins. Co. v. Iriana Ins. Servs., Inc., 2021 WL 8651845, at *6; Am. Int’l Underwriters (Phil.), Inc. v. Cont’l Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253, 1261 (9th Cir. 1988); Transplace Int’l, Inc. v. Motorcar Parts of Am., No. 2:14-cv-07928, 2015 WL 13285088, at *6 (C.D. Cal. Feb. 6, 2015)

quoted_point: On June 10, 2024, the Court granted Lofall’s application for leave to proceed in forma pauperis and ordered Lofall to show cause in writing by July 8, 2024, why the Court should not dismiss his complaint for failure to state a claim.

Plaintiff Tyler Allen Lofall (“Lofall”), a self-represented litigant, filed this action against Defendants State of Oregon, County of Clackamas, Clackamas County Police Department, John Doe Officer 1, and John Doe Officer 2 (together, “Defendants”).
STATE OF OREGON et al., Defendants.
TYLER ALLEN LOFALL, Plaintiff,
DATED this 15th day of July, 2024.

For the reasons stated, the Court recommends that the district judge DISMISS Lofall’s claims against the State of Oregon with prejudice and DISMISS the remainder of Lofall’s claims with leave to amend within thirty days.
If no objections are filed, the Findings and Recommendation will go under advisement on that date.
If objections are filed, a response is due within fourteen (14) days after being served with a copy of the objections.
Objections, if any, are due within fourteen (14) days from service of the Findings and Recommendation.
When the response is due or filed, whichever date is earlier, the Findings and Recommendation will go under advisement.
“A claim has facial plausibility when the plaintiff pleads factual content that allows the court to draw the reasonable inference that the defendant is liable for the misconduct alleged.”
cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009); Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 556 (2007)

quoted_point: “The plausibility standard is not akin to a ‘probability requirement,’ but it asks for more than a sheer possibility that a defendant has acted unlawfully.”
cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009); Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 556 (2007)

quoted_point: A district court must perform a preliminary screening of an in forma pauperis complaint and dismiss any claims that fail to state a claim upon which relief may be granted, are frivolous or malicious, or seek monetary relief against a defendant who is immune from such relief.
cited: 28 U.S.C. § 1915(e)(2)(B); Lopez v. Smith, 203 F.3d 1122, 1129 (9th Cir. 2000); Preciado v. Salas, 2014 WL 127710, at *1 (E.D. Cal. Jan. 14, 2014)

quoted_point: The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure require a plaintiff to state “a short and plain statement” in a complaint showing that the plaintiff is entitled to relief.
To state a claim for relief, a “complaint must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to ‘state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.’”
cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009); Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007)

quoted_point: “In cases involving a [self‑represented] plaintiff, the court construes the pleadings liberally and affords the plaintiff the benefit of any doubt.”
cited: Kali v. Bulk Handling Sys., 2019 WL 1810966, at *4 (D. Or. Apr. 23, 2019); Wolfe v. Strankman, 392 F.3d 358, 362 (9th Cir. 2004)

quoted_point: “Nevertheless, a [self‑represented] plaintiff’s claims may be dismissed without leave to amend where it appears beyond doubt that the plaintiff can prove no set of facts that would entitle [the plaintiff] to relief.”
cited: Graves, 2020 WL 8085140, at *2; Barrett v. Belleque, 544 F.3d 1060, 1061-62 (9th Cir. 2008)

quoted_point: “Pro se pleadings are held to a less stringent standard than those drafted by lawyers.”
cited: Graves v. Nw. Priority Credit Union, 2020 WL 8085140, at *2 (D. Or. Dec. 12, 2020); Haines v. Kerner, 404 U.S. 519, 520 (1972)

quoted_point: “The court, in many circumstances, instructs the [self‑represented] litigant regarding deficiencies in the complaint and grants leave to amend.”
cited: Graves, 2020 WL 8085140, at *2; Eldridge v. Block, 832 F.2d 1132, 1136 (9th Cir. 1987)

quoted_point: Eventually, Lofall was released from jail.
He asserts that he had just been released from jail on bail “for the same issue for which he was . . . re‑arrested[.]”
He describes antagonism and property damage initiated by his ex-girlfriend, and his “false arrest” in March 2022 for harassment.
In his complaint, Lofall alleges that two officers—John Doe Officer 1 and John Doe Officer 2—arrested him in a parking lot on May 24, 2022.
In his declaration, Lofall alleges that the re-arrest subjected him to double jeopardy.
In Lofall’s declaration, he provides background information about an unpaid contract for his remodeling services, legal challenges, and conflict with an ex-girlfriend.
According to Lofall, those people stole the car.
According to the complaint, at the time of his arrest, Lofall was driving his friend’s car.
In his declaration, Lofall states that he was helping a friend recover a stolen vehicle.
Lofall alleges claims of negligence, “deprivation of property under 42 U.S.C. § 1983,” civil theft or conversion, “violation of civil rights,” and intentional infliction of emotional distress.
Lofall also alleges that the officers’ actions resulted in the “loss of [Lofall]’s valuable materials and tools, which were left at [his] friend’s house[.]”
Lofall attached a pro se complaint form to his complaint, which appears to be related to a different case, Lofall v. County of Clackamas, 3:24-cv-00838-SB.
Lofall seeks damages and injunctive relief.
Lofall states that the “West Linn Police officers hid their evidence behind a DHS seal and changed their policy once I filed my claim to sue them.”
Lofall told the arresting officers not to leave the car with other individuals who were present.
The officers disregarded his directions, did not secure Lofall’s property, and gave the other individuals the keys to the car.
“Even though the Oregon Tort Claims Act [“OTCA”] is a waiver of sovereign immunity, it does not waive Eleventh Amendment immunity.”
cited: Olson v. Allen, 2019 WL 1232834, at *5 n.3 (D. Or. Mar. 15, 2019); Ross v. Shelton, 2019 WL 846043, at *7 (D. Or. Feb. 21, 2019)

quoted_point: “The Eleventh Amendment bars suits against the State or its agencies for all types of relief, absent unequivocal consent by the state.”
cited: Romano v. Bible, 169 F.3d 1182, 1185 (9th Cir. 1999); Pennhurst v. Halderman, 465 U.S. 89, 100 (1984)

quoted_point: Accordingly, the Eleventh Amendment bars Lofall’s claims against the State of Oregon, and the Court recommends that the district judge dismiss the State of Oregon with prejudice.
cited: Doyle v. Nevada, 2023 WL 8378945, at *3 (D. Nev. June 9, 2023); Burns v. Hawaii, 2023 WL 2814623, at *3 (D. Haw. Apr. 6, 2023)

quoted_point: Lofall has not alleged that the State of Oregon has waived its Eleventh Amendment immunity or otherwise demonstrated that the State has consented to be sued in federal court.
Police departments are often “merely the vehicle through which [a] city fulfills its police functions,” not “a separate entity from the [c]ity . . . [that is] amenable to suit [under § 1983].”
cited: Shore v. City of Portland, 2018 WL 3469037, at *2 (D. Or. May 21, 2018); Nwerem v. City of Portland, 2006 WL 3228775, at *2 n.1 (D. Or. Nov. 6, 2006)

quoted_point: Section 1983 permits suit against “persons,” which the U.S. Supreme Court has construed to mean “state officials sued in their individual capacities[.]”
The Court ordered Lofall to show cause in writing why the Court should not dismiss his claims against the State of Oregon, and Lofall did not respond.
The state and its agencies “are not ‘persons’ within the meaning of § 1983, and are therefore not amenable to suit under that statute.”
cited: Maldonado v. Harris, 370 F.3d 945, 951 (9th Cir. 2004); Will v. Mich. Dep’t of State Police, 491 U.S. 58, 70 (1989)

quoted_point: “[W]hen deprivations of property are effected through random and unauthorized conduct of a state employee, predeprivation procedures are simply ‘impracticable’ since the state cannot know when such deprivations will occur.”

Accordingly, “an unauthorized intentional deprivation of property by a state employee does not constitute a violation of the procedural requirements of the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment if a meaningful postdeprivation remedy for the loss is available.”
However, Lofall has not alleged that the Clackamas County Police Department is a separate legal entity subject to suit or a proper defendant under Section 1983.
Lofall alleges that Defendants deprived him of his property in violation of the Fourteenth Amendment’s Due Process Clause.
Lofall has properly named Clackamas County and John Doe Officers 1 and 2 as defendants.
The Court ordered Lofall to show cause in writing why the Court should not dismiss his claims against the Clackamas County Police Department, and Lofall did not respond.
The Court ordered Lofall to show cause in writing why the Court should not dismiss his Fourteenth Amendment claim given the available post-deprivation remedy.
The Court recommends that the district judge dismiss Lofall’s claims against the Clackamas County Police Department without prejudice.
cited: Villanueva v. City of Visalia, 2024 WL 1257214, at *1-2 (E.D. Cal. Mar. 25, 2024); Dodds v. City of Eugene, 2023 WL 7166557, at *3 (D. Or. Oct. 31, 2023); Mecautea v. Oregon, 2020 WL 1812012, at *2 (D. Or. Apr. 9, 2020)

quoted_point: Accordingly, Lofall has failed to state a Fourteenth Amendment procedural due process claim, and the Court recommends that the district judge dismiss his Fourteenth Amendment claim without prejudice.
cited: Evans v. Gower, 2022 WL 3226968, at *12 (D. Or. Aug. 10, 2022); Dunbar v. Alameda Cnty. Sheriff’s Off., 2020 WL 2524136, at *1 (N.D. Cal. May 15, 2020)

quoted_point: Further, Lofall has not alleged facts suggesting that he has standing to challenge the loss of his friend’s car and has not alleged sufficient factual matter to demonstrate a causal connection between Defendants’ actions and the loss of his tools from his friend’s house.
Further, the State of Oregon provided Lofall with an adequate post-deprivation remedy—a state tort action.
cited: Rodriguez v. Cain, 2024 WL 1016058, at *5 (D. Or. Mar. 8, 2024)

quoted_point: In his declaration, Lofall mentions that the West Linn Police Department, which is not a defendant to the instant case, changed its policy after his arrest.

Lofall has not alleged that the Clackamas County officers deprived him of his property pursuant to police policy, and the facts alleged do not suggest that “the state could have anticipated the officers’ actions.”
cited: Rodriguez v. Cain, 2024 WL 1016058, at *4 (D. Or. Mar. 8, 2024)

quoted_point: “To state a claim under § 1983, a plaintiff must allege the violation of a right secured by the Constitution and laws of the United States, and must show that the alleged deprivation was committed by a person acting under color of state law.”
Accordingly, the Court recommends that the district judge dismiss Lofall’s claim without prejudice.
In his complaint, Lofall alleges generally that Defendants violated his civil rights.
In his declaration, Lofall suggests a double jeopardy violation.
The Court ordered Lofall to show cause in writing why the Court should not dismiss his violation of civil rights claim for failure to state a claim.
To the extent he suggests that his second arrest was a double jeopardy violation, he has failed to plead sufficient factual matter to establish that he was prosecuted twice for the same offense.
cited: OR. REV. STAT. § 131.515(1); Grant v. State of Or., 2022 WL 2910015, at *6 (D. Or. June 15, 2022)

quoted_point: To the extent that Lofall alleges a general civil rights violation, the complaint is too vague to state a claim.
cited: Calderon v. Covello, 2023 WL 7168471, at *3 (E.D. Cal. Oct. 31, 2023)

quoted_point: “[F]ailure to plead that notice of claims was given in accordance with the OTCA subjects a complaint to dismissal.”
cited: Harris v. City of Portland Police Dep’t, 2016 WL 890927, at *3 (D. Or. Mar. 8, 2016); Halseth v. Deines, 2004 WL 1919994, at *3 (D. Or. Aug. 26, 2004)

quoted_point: Accordingly, the Court recommends that the district judge dismiss Lofall’s state law claims without prejudice.
cited: Wilson v. Dep’t of Hum. Servs., 2021 WL 6618288, at *2-3 (D. Or. Nov. 4, 2021)

quoted_point: However, Lofall has not pled timely notice under the OTCA.

However, the Court notes that Lofall has an ongoing state court case related to those events (see Clackamas County Circuit Court No. 22CV39627), and therefore the Younger abstention doctrine (which bars a federal court from interfering with ongoing state court proceedings absent extraordinary circumstances) and the Rooker-Feldman doctrine (which bars a federal court from hearing a direct appeal from the final judgment of a state court) will likely bar Lofall’s claims relating to his pending state case.
Lofall alleges state law claims of negligence, conversion, and intentional infliction of emotional distress.
The Court ordered Lofall to show cause in writing why the Court should not dismiss his state law claims for failure to plead timely notice, and Lofall did not respond.

To the extent Lofall raises new claims based on events described in his declaration, those claims are not part of Lofall’s current complaint and the Court will consider those claims only if Lofall elects to include them in an amended complaint.

NOTICE IS HEREBY GIVEN that Plaintiff-Appellant TYLER ALLEN LOFALL appeals to the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit from the following: 1. The Opinion and Order entered September 3, 2025 (ECF 60). 2. The Order entered October 3, 2025 denying Plaintiff’s post-judgment motion under Rules 59(e) and 60(b) (ECF 63). 3. Any prior interlocutory orders that merged into the final judgment.
This Court has jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1291.
This notice is filed within 30 days of the October 3, 2025 order disposing of Plaintiff’s post-judgment motion.
Plaintiff requests a short extension of time to file a notice of appeal so the Ninth Circuit can address the merits.
The brief delay arose from a good-faith misdirection of a late-night filing to the related docket, corrected the next morning, with no prejudice.
Under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a)(5)(A), the Court may extend the time to file a notice of appeal upon a showing of excusable neglect or good cause.
The Pioneer factors, applied in the Ninth Circuit by Pincay and Briones, guide this equitable determination.
cited: Pioneer Inv. Servs. Co. v. Brunswick, Pincay v. Andrews, Briones v. Riviera Hotel

quoted_point: The delay was measured in hours.
Plaintiff alerted counsel/the Court within about an hour and re-filed correctly the next morning.
Extending the time promotes adjudication on the merits consistent with Ninth Circuit precedent.
On October 1, 2025 at approximately 11:57 PM Pacific Time, I attempted to file a Rule 59(e)/60(b) motion but it was inadvertently filed in the related remanded case (3:24-cv-00838-SB).
Within approximately one hour (around 1:06 AM on October 2, 2025), I notified opposing counsel and the Court by email that the filing had gone to the wrong case number and provided the papers.
On the morning of October 2, 2025 (approximately 8:48 AM), I re-filed the motion on the correct docket for this case (3:24-cv-00839-SB).
On October 8, 2025, the Court stated it lacked discretion to treat my Rule 59(e) motion as timely under Rule 6(b) and declined nunc pro tunc relief (ECF 65).
Opposing parties received electronic notice of my filings through CM/ECF close in time to the events above.
The Court has jurisdiction over Lofall’s claims pursuant to 28 U.S.C. §§ 1331 and 1367, and all parties have consented to the jurisdiction of a magistrate judge pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 636.
For the reasons explained below, the Court grants Defendants’ motions.
Lofall alleges that while in jail, officials denied him medical care and sabotaged his legal files in an effort to interfere with his ability to defend himself.
On November 18, 2022, Lofall filed a complaint in the Clackamas County Circuit Court asserting several claims against Defendants and others based on the same underlying events at issue in this case.

On June 4, 2024, the Court sua sponte remanded the case to Clackamas County Circuit Court on the ground that “[t]he right to remove a state court case to federal court is clearly limited to defendants.”
cited: Am. Int’l Underwriters v. Cont’l Ins. Co. (AIU), 843 F.2d 1253, 1260 (9th Cir. 1988)

quoted_point: Where, as here, the defendant asserts a facial challenge to jurisdiction, a district court evaluates that challenge under the familiar Rule 12(b)(6) standard.
cited: Leite v. Crane Co., 749 F.3d 1117 (9th Cir. 2014); Bowen v. Energizer Holdings, Inc., 118 F.4th 1134 (9th Cir. 2024)

quoted_point: “The right to remove a state court case to federal court is clearly limited to defendants.”
A plaintiff who commences his action in a state court cannot effectuate removal to a federal court even if he could have originated the action in a federal court.
cited: Oregon Egg Producers v. Andrew, 458 F.2d 382, 383 (9th Cir. 1972)

quoted_point: The Ninth Circuit has instructed that “[h]aving elected state court, plaintiff should be bound by its choice absent compelling reasons to seek relief in another forum.”

Allowing Lofall to litigate his claims in state court until the eve of trial and then start over and litigate the same claims against the same defendants in federal court would waste judicial resources and result in prejudice to Defendants by duplicating the costs of defense.

All of the claims in Lofall’s SAC are time-barred because more than two years elapsed before he pleaded those claims in this case, the claims do not relate back to his original complaint, and the various equitable doctrines on which Lofall relies do not apply.
The Court GRANTS Defendants’ motions to dismiss (ECF Nos. 34, 36, and 37), and DISMISSES this case for lack of federal subject matter jurisdiction.
The Court dismisses the case without prejudice but without further leave to amend.

City Defendants move to dismiss each and every claim against them in the Second Amended Complaint for failure to state claims upon which relief can be granted; for being filed outside of the statute of limitations, and for being filed in violation of the Court’s Order dated August 7, 2024.
All claims now brought by Plaintiff based upon these alleged facts are barred by the applicable statute of limitations.
Plaintiff’s operative Complaint violates this Court’s prior order, both by being untimely and in that Plaintiff lacked authority from this Court to name additional defendants in this action.
West Linn Police Department is a city department and therefore is not [a proper party] under § 1983.
cited: United States v. Kama, 394 F.3d 1236; Thomas v. Oregon, 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 40741

quoted_point: Plaintiff’s Second Amended Complaint fails to allege more than conclusory statements/opinions as facts underlying his numerous constitutional claims against City Defendants.
cited: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662; Bell Atl. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544

quoted_point: Municipal defendants cannot be held liable when no constitutional violation has occurred.
cited: Los Angeles v. Heller, 475 U.S. 796; Scott v. Henrich, 39 F.3d 912

quoted_point: Plaintiff lacks standing to seek any injunctive relief as there is no plausible allegation of a real or immediate threat that Plaintiff will suffer any further harm by City Defendants.

quoted_point: Plaintiff’s Second Amended Complaint fails to allege any facts that City Defendants acted with “evil motive or intent,” or “reckless or callous indifference,” necessary for punitive damages.

quoted_point: Defendant Deputy District Attorney Rebecca Portlock respectfully moves to dismiss her from this action pursuant to Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6).
When a prosecutor’s actions are “intimately associated with the judicial phase of the criminal process,” the doctrine of absolute immunity applies with full force.
A prosecutor’s decision not to preserve or provide exculpatory evidence falls within a prosecutor’s discretion as an advocate, as do statements made in court proceedings.
cited: Broam v. Bogan, 320 F.3d 1023; Imbler, 424 U.S. at 431

quoted_point: All of Plaintiff’s claims against the DDA Portlock arise from the prosecution of Plaintiff and that conduct which is intimately associated with the judicial process.
His cause of action began to accrue, at the latest, in July 2022, when DDA Portlock dismissed Plaintiff’s criminal case.
By [September 9, 2024], the two‑year statute of limitations had expired.
cited: Douglas v. Noelle, 567 F.3d 1103, 1109 (9th Cir. 2009); ORS 12.110

quoted_point: The Court committed clear legal error by treating the prudential AIU doctrine as a jurisdictional bar under Rule 12(b)(1), by applying abstention when no parallel state case existed, and by dismissing rather than staying a damages action in violation of Quackenbush v. Allstate Ins. Co., 517 U.S. 706 (1996).
The Ninth Amendment is a rule of construction: courts must not construe enumerated rights and procedures in a way that denies or disparages other rights retained by the people.
cited: U.S. Const. amend. IX; Strandberg v. City of Helena, 791 F.2d 744, 748 (9th Cir. 1986)

quoted_point: On September 3, 2025, this Court dismissed Plaintiff's Second Amended Complaint under Rule 12(b)(1) as a “repetitive lawsuit,” citing American Int'l Underwriters (Philippines), Inc. v. Continental Ins. Co., 843 F.2d 1253 (9th Cir. 1988).
That dismissal was procured through Defendants' fraud and rests on manifest legal and factual error.

A Rule 59(e) motion may be granted to “(1) correct manifest errors of law or fact, (2) present newly discovered evidence, (3) account for an intervening change in controlling law, or (4) prevent manifest injustice.”
cited: Allstate Ins. Co. v. Herron, 634 F.3d 1101, 1111 (9th Cir. 2011)

quoted_point: Rule 60(b)(3) permits relief for “fraud... misrepresentation, or misconduct by an opposing party.”
Fraud upon the court is “an unconscionable plan or scheme which is designed to improperly influence the court in its decision.”
cited: In re Intermagnetics Am., Inc., 926 F.2d 912, 917 (9th Cir. 1991)

quoted_point: This Court had mandatory federal‑question jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1331 and diversity jurisdiction under § 1332.
When jurisdiction exists, abstention is discretionary—and in a damages action, the remedy (if any) is a stay, not dismissal.
cited: Quackenbush v. Allstate Ins. Co., 517 U.S. 706, 721 (1996)
